From WeWork to We=C2=A0
When he met McKelvey, Adam Neumann had run two failed companies: one that m=
ade a collapsible heel for women=E2=80=99s shoes, another that made baby cl=
othing with knee pads. Both seem like Shark Tank pitches that would have go=
tten five =E2=80=9CI=E2=80=99m outs.=E2=80=9D=C2=A0
In half a decade, though, Neumann had built the largest coworking company i=
n the world, thrown the traditional five-to-ten-year lease model into quest=
ion, and raised over $1 billion dollars. His company controlled millions of=
 square feet of office. Given that meteoric rise, it=E2=80=99s understandab=
le that Neumann thought he could do anything.=C2=A0
It started with WeLive. If coworking worked, why couldn=E2=80=99t coliving?=
 Theoretically, the same principles apply: lease space wholesale, chop it u=
p and add shared amenities, and sell it retail. Plus, maybe if we all lived=
 together, we could cure loneliness and make people happier. In the company=
=E2=80=99s Series D deck, it said, =E2=80=9CThe demand for Space as a Servi=
ce extends to residential real estate, making WeLive a natural extension of=
 the WeWork concept, community, and brand.=E2=80=9D
Series D investors bought it, as did investors in the company=E2=80=99s $43=
3 million May 2015 Series E and its $690 million November 2016 Series F. Bu=
t no one bought Neumann=E2=80=99s vision to be more than just office space =
than his Series G lead: Softbank=E2=80=99s Masa Son.=C2=A0

1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">This is the W=
eWork enigma. It has done some truly mind-blowingly impressive things while=
 burning mountains of Masa’s money. If it weren’t for some huge=

 way back in 2019, <em>before</em> COVID supersized remote work valuations.=
 It rode a positive narrative to the moon, and came crashing down when the =
narrative flipped. </p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; li=
ne-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">We all know We. The company’s st=
ory was everywhere in the fall of 2019. WeWork’s failed IPO was a car=

nfreude (this is Packy, speaking for myself). </p><p style=3D"color: #=
1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">But since i=
ts public failure, it installed new management, flew under the radar, and r=
efocused on its core business. It’s less exciting, but more solid. It=
s business model is neither inherently terrible nor is it as high-margin, h=
igh-growth as a pure software business. </p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a=
; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">When WeWork was p=

px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">For the first eight years of WeW=

px; margin: 0;">He regularly called his direct reports’ direct report=

i><li style=3D"margin: 3.75px 0 3.75px 7.5px; mso-special-format: bullet;">=

more power. </p></li><li style=3D"margin: 3.75px 0 3.75px 7.5px; mso-s=
pecial-format: bullet;"><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-h=
eight: 26px; margin: 0;">He personally trademarked “We” and sol=

gin: 3.75px 0 3.75px 7.5px; mso-special-format: bullet;"><p style=3D"color:=
 #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0;">He established wh=

><strong>perfect</strong></em> person to launch WeWork, shake up the sleepy=
 commercial real estate industry, and change the way that people rent offic=
e space.</p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px;=
 margin: 0 0 1em 0;">Commercial real estate isn’t like software, a fa=
ct that critics pointed out over and over again as WeWork’s valuation=
 soared to $47 billion. In the world of bits, a polite, meek coder can spen=
d months locked away in a dark room, emerge with a brilliant piece of softw=
are, put it up for sale on a website that anyone can access, and let the ma=
rket decide whether what they built is worth paying for. It’s pretty =
close to a meritocracy. </p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16p=
x; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">Commercial real estate is an olig=
opoly. A handful of families, companies, and Real Estate Investment Trusts =
(REITs) own the vast majority of the desirable real estate in the world&#82=
17;s major metropolises. They own buildings and collect rent checks. Life i=
s pretty good if you own the right piece of real estate, so landlords are n=
ot known for being particularly forward thinking or open to change. Polite =
and meek don’t work in real estate.</p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; fo=
nt-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">Adam Neumann almost s=
unk his company, but he was also the right person, in the right place, at t=
he right time to get it off the ground in the first place. </p><p styl=
e=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;=
">With the benefit of hindsight, it’s easy to say that WeWork should =
have done all of the good stuff and none of the bad stuff. They should have=
 done revenue share deals with landlords instead of taking on lease obligat=
ions. They shouldn’t have thrown big parties. Adam should have been m=
ore straightforward and honest. </p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-s=
ize: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">But you can’t have =
your cake and eat it too. A quieter, more conservative WeWork wouldn’=
t have had the scale, money, shine, or leverage to make a dent in commercia=
l real estate. </p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-h=
eight: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">WeWork was the disruptor. Like Napster, it=
 broke some rules. Normally, the disruptor makes a lot of noise, spends a l=
ot of money, and fails. Someone else swoops into a shaken industry, works w=
ith landlords, and builds a solid business. </p><p style=3D"color: #1a=
1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">But in this s=
tory, Adam met Masa. SoftBank’s billions both choked WeWork and kept =
it alive. Adam got the boot, and rightly so. His company, under new managem=
ent, lives to fight another day. </p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-=
size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">What’s unique abou=
t this story is that WeWork is both Napster and Spotify. It’s disrupt=
